title: NOC 74201 – Water transport deck and engine room crew | GTR Immigration
description: NOC 74201 professionals are the essential operational heart of Canada's maritime industry. As a TEER 4 occupation, this group includes deckhands and engine room crew who operate and maintain vessels ranging from massive container ships and tankers to ferries and tugboats. In 2026, as Canada executes its "Blue Economy Strategy" and expands its Arctic and coastal shipping routes, these maritime workers are classified as Critical Supply Chain Talent. For immigration, this role is a premier candidate for the Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP) and the BC PNP Entry-Level and Semi-Skilled (ELSS) stream.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category7 – Trades, transport and equipment operators and related occupationsTEER Level4Major Group74 – Mail and message distribution, transport equipment operators and maintenanceSub-Major Group742 – Transport equipment operators, utility maintenance and related workersMinor Group7420 – Transport equipment operators, utility maintenance and related workersNOC Code74201

duties: Deck Operations: Assist in the mooring and unmooring of vessels; operate winches and cranes to load and unload cargo and supplies.
Watchkeeping: Maintain a lookout for other vessels, navigational hazards, and icebergs; assist the officer of the watch in navigating the vessel.
Maintenance: Chip rust, paint, and clean decks and superstructures; splice ropes and wire cables; maintain lifeboats and firefighting equipment.
Engine Room Support: (Engine Crew) Assist marine engineers in the operation and maintenance of main engines, generators, and auxiliary machinery.
Monitoring: Lubricate moving parts of engines; monitor gauges and record readings in the engine room log; assist in fuel and water bunkering operations.
Safety & Security: Participate in fire and boat drills; ensure all safety protocols are followed during the transit and handling of hazardous cargo.
employment_requirements: Completion of secondary school is usually required.

Certifications (Mandatory):Bridge Watch Rating or Engine Room Rating certificate from Transport Canada.STCW Basic Safety Training (MED - Marine Emergency Duties).Marine Medical Certificate issued by a Transport Canada-approved physician.
Bridge Watch Rating or Engine Room Rating certificate from Transport Canada.
STCW Basic Safety Training (MED - Marine Emergency Duties).
Marine Medical Certificate issued by a Transport Canada-approved physician.
Experience: While entry-level deckhand roles are available, most senior rating roles require documented sea-time (typically 6–12 months).
Skills: Physical strength, ability to work in confined spaces or at heights, and the capacity to work on "Rotational Shifts" (e.g., 28 days on, 28 days off) are essential.
salary_prospects: Note: In 2026, experienced Bosuns or Oilers working on deep-sea tankers or in the Arctic often earn annual salaries between $75,000 and $105,000 inclusive of sea-pay premiums and overtime.
job_outlook: The outlook is Favourable. Projections indicate that the maritime sector will face a significant "Talent Gap" as nearly 30% of the current workforce reaches retirement age by 2030. The shift toward "Digital Maritime Twin" technology and automated engine monitoring will require crew members to have higher levels of technical literacy, ensuring long-term job security for the next generation of seafarers.
labour_market_demand: The demand for deck and engine room crew in 2026 is Very Good, particularly in coastal provinces and the Great Lakes region. In British Columbia and Nova Scotia, the expansion of the National Shipbuilding Strategy and increased liquid natural gas (LNG) exports have created a persistent labor shortage. According to 2026 Transport Canada reports, there is a specific urgent need for crew members with STCW (Standards of Training, Certification, and Watchkeeping) endorsements to support international and cross-border shipping routes.
Global Maritime Excellence: Work with some of the world's most modern and environmentally friendly vessel fleets, including hybrid-electric ferries and ice-strengthened tankers.
Exceptional Career Progression: Start as a deckhand and progress toward becoming a Marine Engineer (NOC 21310) or a Deck Officer (NOC 72600) through sea-time and certification.
Competitive Wages & Benefits: Marine crew roles often offer high hourly rates, significant overtime, and "Live-on-Board" arrangements that include meals and accommodation.
Strategic PR Pathways: This TEER 4 role is explicitly targeted by Provincial Nominee Programs (PNP) in the Atlantic and Pacific regions, offering a streamlined route to Permanent Residency.
pathways: Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP): A high-conversion route for crew members with a job offer from a designated employer in the Atlantic provinces.
BC PNP Entry-Level and Semi-Skilled (ELSS): A direct route for deckhands and oilers currently employed in British Columbia for at least 9 months.
New 2026 TR to PR Pathway: A dedicated stream for 33,000 workers already in Canada, targeting essential transport and supply chain roles.
Rural Community Immigration Pilot (RCIP): Ideal for those willing to work in port-dependent communities in Ontario or the Prairies.
